title: US Deploys Small Modular Reactors: Balancing Risk and Opportunity | Tier Tech International, Inc. posted on the topic | LinkedIn
text:
The U.S. wants to deploy advanced nuclear reactor- Small Modular Reactors for DOW installations, next-gen plants for grid resilience. One problem: most of these designs require HALEU (High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium). There’s no domestic commercial production. Until recently, Russia was our primary supplier. Now consider the stakeholders: - Nonproliferation advocates want to slow down until safeguards mature - Energy security proponents want to accelerate at any cost - DOW program managers need fuel certainty for installation timelines - Private developers need supply commitments to secure financing Same decision. Four completely different lenses. Without structured integration, this becomes a political battle. Whoever has the most organizational influence- or the loudest voice- wins. With structured risk and opportunity mapping through our Risk-Informed Opportunity Sequences (RIOS) process, the conversation changes: The RIOS Output: Downblend stockpiles for interim supply, secure allied agreements, accelerate domestic enrichment, phase commercial deployment. Each addresses specific risks or captures specific opportunities. Decision-makers can combine and sequence these based on priorities- and re-sequence as conditions change. #RIOS #RiskInformedOpportunitySequences #StrategicDesign #RiskManagement #OpportunityPursuit #DecisionMaking

      Carbonium Core will be the first, and at present, the only domestic supplier of nuclear-grade graphite to support the next-generation reactors. Carbonium owns and controls the entire supply chain from mine to manufacture, including fabrication of client-specified components. Based in East Tennessee, Carbonium will be able to supply the highest-quality product at lower price points than offshore suppliers currently charge, supporting our domestic push for small modular and micro reactors. For more information, contact Brad Pearman, VP of Carbonium Core. 🚨 Smartkem Welcomes State-Level Backing for U.S. Nuclear Graphite Manufacturing Momentum is building behind Carbonium Core’s proposed domestic nuclear-grade graphite manufacturing facility in Tennessee with strong support from state leadership. Tennessee State Senator Jessie Seal said: “This upcoming manufacturing unit is a major win for our local community. This development will bring many quality jobs, outstanding economic growth, and new opportunities for our communities. I would like to offer my full support to Carbonium Core, Inc. and I look forward to working closely with them to ensure everyone’s long-term success.” If completed, the proposed transaction between Smartkem and Carbonium Core would bring together advanced materials and process innovation with a vertically integrated nuclear graphite platform, helping establish a secure, domestic supply of a material critical to next-generation nuclear technologies, including SMRs and Generation IV reactors. This is about: - Strengthening U.S. energy security - Onshoring advanced manufacturing - Building resilient domestic supply chains Read the full announcement: https://ir.smartkem.com/ #Smartkem #NuclearGraphite #AdvancedMaterials #EnergySecurity #USManufacturing #CleanEnergy #SMRs To view or add a comment, sign in

      Texas is becoming one of the nation's primary testing grounds for small modular nuclear reactors. The driver is simple: demand on the Texas grid could nearly triple by 2050, fueled by data centers, EVs, and Permian Basin electrification. Wind and solar cannot carry that load alone. Texas needs firm, dispatchable power. Several projects are moving from concept to reality. X-energy plans four 80-megawatt reactors at Dow Chemical's Seadrift plant, backed by $1.2 billion from the Department of Energy. Austin-based Aalo Atomics plans to activate its first test reactor within months. A project in Abilene targets late 2026 or early 2027 for its research reactor to go online. The economics remain the real test. Current cost projections for SMRs run well above what makes them competitive in the ERCOT market today. Regulatory reform, construction efficiency, and long-term power purchase agreements from data centers will be key to closing that gap. For manufacturers evaluating Texas expansion, this is a story worth tracking. Reliable, affordable, dispatchable power is one of the core reasons Texas has led the nation in business attraction for 13 consecutive years. If SMRs prove out here, that advantage grows. At Jetton Solutions, energy and infrastructure planning is central to how we guide clients through Texas expansion decisions. Texas Tribune, Feb. 17, 2026: https://lnkd.in/e7FURE4W #Texas #Energy #NuclearEnergy #SMR #Manufacturing #EconomicDevelopment #SiteSelection #JettonSolutions To view or add a comment, sign in

      Centrus just locked in Fluor as EPC contractor for its multi-billion dollar uranium enrichment expansion in Ohio, a major signal that US domestic fuel capacity is moving from pilot scale to industrial deployment. Scope: • Expands large-scale LEU production to address a $2.3B backlog • Builds 12 metric tons per year HALEU capacity for advanced reactors • Strengthens US nuclear fuel security and supply chain resilience • Aligns with DOE and NNSA priorities for domestic enrichment To view or add a comment, sign in

      America just restarted its only production-scale uranium recovery facility after decades of dormancy. H Canyon at South Carolina's Savannah River Site represents a strategic game-changer for US nuclear independence. The numbers are compelling: 19 metric tons of HALEU production potential, enough fuel for multiple advanced reactor projects, and a fall 2027 target for first deliveries. Why this matters now: Advanced reactors require HALEU (5-20% enriched uranium) instead of conventional reactor fuel. Until now, America relied heavily on foreign sources for this critical material. H Canyon transforms previous "waste" materials into high-value reactor fuel through sophisticated chemical separation processes. Strategic advantages include: domestic supply security for advanced reactors, reduced foreign dependencies, support for small modular reactor development, and enhanced nonproliferation objectives. The optimisation opportunity: Research shows using LEU+ blendstock instead of natural uranium could boost HALEU output by 3 additional metric tons from the same feedstock. This facility enables America's nuclear renaissance by providing reliable fuel supplies that advanced reactor developers need for investment confidence. The timing aligns perfectly with growing demand as multiple advanced reactor technologies approach commercial deployment in the late 2020s. Enjoy this summary? Hit the 'like' button to let us know and follow this page to stay up to date on nuclear energy developments. Want to know more? This helps pave the way for increased nuclear fuel production for North America's growing fleet of reactors.
